claim: Agents are LLMs running in a loop with tool use—the LLM takes output, feeds it back, and makes decisions on what prompt to run next, when to call tools, and when to complete
actionable: Design agents as loops where the LLM coordinates tool calls and self-corrects; implement feedback loops so the agent refines its approach based on tool outputs
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Use pydantic BaseModel to define structured output schemas; pass these schemas to the LLM prompt so it generates responses matching your required data types
actionable: Create pydantic models defining desired output fields (topic, summary, sources, tools_used); use these to constrain LLM output to structured, usable Python objects
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Tools are Python functions wrapped with metadata (name, description); the agent selects and calls tools based on task requirements and function descriptions
actionable: Wrap any Python function as a tool using tool() decorator; provide clear descriptions so the agent understands when to call each tool
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Prompt templates with partial() allow you to pre-fill format instructions based on pydantic schema; this tells the LLM exactly how to structure its response
actionable: Use ChatPromptTemplate with partial() to inject format_instructions derived from your pydantic model; ensures consistent, parseable LLM output
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Virtual environments (venv) isolate project dependencies; always activate venv before pip installing to avoid conflicts with global Python packages
actionable: Create venv with 'python -m venv venv'; activate it (source venv/bin/activate on Mac/Linux, venv\Scripts\activate on Windows); install dependencies into isolated environment
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Environment variables (.env file with load_dotenv) safely store API keys without committing them to version control
actionable: Create .env with OPENAI_API_KEY and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY; use load_dotenv() to load them; never commit .env to git
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Lang chain's create_tool_calling_agent() and agent_executor simplify agent setup; pass agent, tools, and verbose flag; invoke with agent_executor.invoke()
actionable: Use create_tool_calling_agent(llm, prompt, tools) to create agent; wrap with AgentExecutor(agent, tools, verbose=True); call via executor.invoke()
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Tool descriptions are critical; vague descriptions lead to poor tool selection; clear descriptions tell the agent when and why to use each tool
actionable: Write descriptive tool descriptions (not just names); include context on when/why the agent should call each tool
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Free tools like Wikipedia and DuckDuckGo can be wrapped as agents without API keys; commercial APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) require API keys but have usage costs
actionable: Start with free search tools (Wikipedia, DuckDuckGo) for prototyping; add commercial LLM API keys (OpenAI, Anthropic) for production agents
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners

claim: Wrap the agent output in try-except blocks; models can fail to return correct schema; catch exceptions and handle gracefully with fallback responses
actionable: Always wrap parsing with try-except to catch schema mismatches; log the raw response and error for debugging
source_title: Build an AI Agent From Scratch in Python - Tutorial for Beginners
